speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 4. I'm Rhoda Daum-Kenner. Today is June-- I keep forgetting the date-- June 9, 1995. And we are talking with survivor Norman Salsitz. And we're doing this interview in English, and we're doing it in, in Springfield, New Jersey. Norman, we were just talking about the, the powerful letter that you received from your mother, which, together with your father's parting words to you upon his execution carried you for, as you said, the last 50 years of your life and are still there as a driving force in your life. Can you tell us what happened when you finished destroying the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- when-- why I'm sitting here, and why I'm going out and I talk and talk and talk-- this is my mother's legacy. I have to tell and retell the stories. Because if we wouldn't tell the story, somebody else would write it for us and it will be all wrong. When we came to our-- to the camp, we started to destroy the, the, the ghetto. And, house by house-- and this was very painful. We knew the people who lived there. And we took it apart. And when we threw down the things from the attics-- so then, later, we could see impor-- how poor the people were. They never threw out anything. Things that they would own 200 years. Old beds. Old bathtubs. Everything they kept, they kept, and we threw it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? Did you burn everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For the Germans, this was very important, because for everything it was old material. And for the Germans, old material they recycled for new material.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took one house, and in this house we have-- every room had something else. One room, it had iron, and one copper, one silver, one books, and so on. And the wood that we took apart was given to the-- to the Polish people, they should have wood for winter. And we worked there every day. I ha--

speaker: interviewee
text: From this camp, I have to tell you, we didn't have no guards. Because we were respons-- we didn't have where to run away anymore, so we were responsible for each other. And this Fardonye said, if somebody would run away, 10 others will be hanged. And this was old people. There were people from my town. And nobody would dare to run away, to have his 10 friends be killed. So we were-- And also, we knew that every place we run away, we'll be killed. And here, at least, we had good food. Because they-- in this camp that we were, there were-- this was the place where there were, before the war, about 10 Jewish bakers. And among us there were Jewish bakers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans gave us the flour for the whole population, to bake bread for the town's population. So naturally they baked the bread, and we stole a lot of bread. So we had enough bread for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not only bread. When I-- and I was the one that was picked out to go to Rzeszow and to bring this old material-- this iron and every scrap. And what I-- I made a double bottom in the wagon. And every time when I went, I took along with me 25 loaves of bread, on this bottom, and covered on top with old junk-- bottles and other things. And I brought this to Rzeszow. And there were some people from my town in Rzeszow. And I would distribute to them-- for them, it was a wonderful thing. They had their bread. And this went on for quite a bit. Then also-- then later, I was appointed to work, by this Fardonye in a warehouse. The warehouse was very-- everything that they took away from the Jews during all the time, it was in this warehouse. Finally, he wanted to get rid of it. And he found there lot of stuffs that they needed in Germany, like coffee and soap and oil and cocoa and chocolate. And they didn't have it. So he-- And those things were from my father's store. And on the boxes was written--

speaker: interviewer
text: What an irony.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the boxes was written 'Isak Salsitz.' When they liquidated, when they took away everything when I ran away to Russia-- and this was laying there. So he asked, who will know about the merchandise? So he was told that there is a son. So they took me, every day, to this warehouse, and I made packages. During lunch hours, I came back with the other men. And I had a can of 50 liter. And we stopped in a milk place where the peasants used to bring milk to make butter for the Germans. And the skim milk was given out to people. So we received 50 quarts of skim milk to bring into our camp. One day, I took the 50 quarts of milk. And there was a woman. And I didn't have to stay in line, because I had to go back to work. And I didn't stay in line. She said, if you don't stay in line, I will pour out  the milk. So then she run to me, she poured out the milk, and I told her something. And she had a boyfriend. She was a very loose woman. She had a boyfriend who was the head of the post office-- a German. And she told him that I threatened her when they-- if the Russians will come in I would kill her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German went to Fardonye. He told him this. And Fardonye went to the Polish police, and he said, tomorrow when Salsitz will come to work-- because I went to work every day. When Salsitz come to work, you take him and you shoot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Polish commandant, and the Polish commandant was an antisemite. His name was Patyk-- P-A-T-Y-K. He was antisemite. He came, and he told me, tomorrow we have to shoot you. You better run away. Why he told me this, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, right away-- and he said to me, did you have a quarrel with somebody by the milk? I said yes. Right away, I went to some people who knew this Fardonye to see if we can bribe him or something. Nobody wanted to do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was the head of the Kripo-- Kriminalpolizei. He was a Ukrainian who was a very good friend with my father. Before the war, my father used to give him, from the holiday, sugar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, nobody can do anything. But run away. He told me to run. I said, how can I run away? They will kill-- they will kill 10 others! He said, you and brother run away. Why do you care about somebody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, I will not run away. I don't want somebody should be killed. He said, take along your brother. So I said, 20 will be killed. I came back, and I told this story to all our boys. And I told them, they were afraid I will run away, I said, I will not run away. I didn't run away. The next day, I go to work. And then I come in where they let me in. Two policeman are staying there, and they call me. And one showed me a shovel in the corner. He said, take the shovel and come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew what the story is. When this policeman, this Patyk, saw me coming, he was white like, like, like, like snow. He figured, why did you come? And they took me out, and I, uh-- and they said to me, start to dig. I start to dig. I said, what  am I digging for? Who? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dig-- don't ask questions. It was already till my knees, till my-- till here! It was deep enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, tell me, who is was going to be shot here? And why I'm there. They said, you. I said, why? Don't ask questions. In the last minute, what do you do? And you know what some people say-- what is the taste of death? How if-- I knew the taste of death, because I dug and I knew that I would be shot in five minutes or two minutes. So I didn't know what to do. To take a shovel of sand and to throw it in his face and run away-- one was staying to one side, the other was staying in the other side, so he would shoot me. They was standing with rifles. So then something came in my mind, and I said that Fardonye had a secretary-- a girl-- a, a Polish girl from my town, and I knew her. They said, go and bring Krisha Kotelecka. This was her name. I have something to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, we cannot go. We have to shoot you. I said, you will shoot me anyway. Go. They went, and they brought Krisha Kotelecka. When she came, she started to cross herself. So I said, I need a favor. You go to Fardonye and tell him I have something to tell him. She said, I cannot go-- he will shoot me! I said, go. She went, and she told him that I am in the hole and I want to speak to him. This Fardonye was very shrewd. He always wanted to have somebody should tell him about somebody else. So he figured, in the last minute I am going to be-- I will tell him that I know stories, I know secrets, I know the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, let him come over-- come over to the office. He said, you have a nerve to ask-- what do you want? And I came with the two policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I want to talk to you nobody should hear. So I said, whoa, whoa! So I said, I want to talk to you. He took me in this other place. And I said to him, I know you like bohnenkaffee-- you like coffee beans. I can give you bohnenkaffee if you will let me out. He jumped, and he took out his revolver. He said to me, You verfluchte Jude, I'm going to shoot you! I said, if you will shoot me here-- and then I was not afraid anymore! I said, you shoot me here, at least you shoot me under a roof. And there they're going to shoot me in a hole. I said to her-- and I said--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he mellowed. He said, where you have bohnenkaffee? I said, I don't have. I had, in this time, hidden 100 kilo unroasted bohnenkaffee-- not roasted. So I said, I don't have bohnenkaffee, but I know a lot of people, and I can go to them, and I can buy and bring it to you. Little by little, we started to bargain. He said, how much will you give me? He says, 50 kilo. I says, 50 kilo? You cannot find in all Poland. I offered him five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we bargained. We came out to 25 kilo.

speaker: interviewer
text: To save your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to, to let me go. Then he wrote down, in his little book, '25 kilo.' He wrote down. Then I said to me, what will I do? I let you out, and you will run away? I said, I could run away yesterday, because I knew yesterday you're going to shoot me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, you-- who told you this? Who told you? You have to tell me. I said, I wouldn't tell you. If somebody wants to save me, what we-- can you do? You can shoot me-- that's all. I would not-- he saved me. Why didn't you run away? I said, because you will kill 10 others. So for this reason you didn't run away? So I ask him, wouldn't you do it, not to run away, save your life, and not to have 10 your friends be killed? He didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, now I need the passierschein. And this is what I  gave you now, this document. He made me out this, and I have to go to Rzeszow. I can go--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I give you this-- for  how long? I said, a half a year. He said, no. 30 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: We bargained. And every month he prolonged it. And I started to go to Rzeszow, every week, three times. And I brought-- I had the coffee. I gave him 10 deca, a half a kilo, little-- and every time I gave him, he write it-- wrote it down. And this went on-- this went on till-- and we worked till we knew that we would be liquidated. There was no more work. The day this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had buried the coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not have-- no, no. I had it by a peasant. and he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the peasant you talked about-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not. But this other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Another peasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he-- and I had a pass to go out to him. This went on until the 18th of September. And the 18th of September, this same Patyk, the same Polish policeman, came, while we-- in the evening. And he said to me, listen, you didn't listen to me the first time I wanted to save you. Tonight we're going to liquidate you-- all of you. The German police and the Polish [INAUDIBLE]. You run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, why are you doing it? I said, I have a reason. I went to tell my people to-- that this happened. Nobody believed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look, this man warned me once before. He warns me the second [INAUDIBLE]. So I started to-- now before I started to organize for escape, because I knew that eventually we'll escape. Because I knew we'll not be there in winter, because Fardonye, the man, didn't leave the wood from the houses for us. Only we gave it away to the Poles. And he knew that in the winter we will need wood. So I knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, I'll make sure. I go to this policeman home. And I asked him, tell me, why do you want to save me? I know you don't like Jews. Why do you want to save me? His wife was there, so he said, now, you promise you will run away? I said yes, I am ready to run away. So I said, I will tell you. I had a son-- a only son. In 1939, he was in the army. He was killed. And he came from a other town. And when I came to this town, and when I saw you the first time, I almost got a heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: You look so much like my son. And I said, this is the same guy as my son. I came home, I told it to my wife. And my wife asked me to bring her to you. And she-- he brought her to our store. In this time I was still at the store. And when she saw me, she started to cry. And she said to him, you have to promise me-- if there will be a time that you could save him, make believe you save your son. And this is the reason I save you. And I want you should run away. I came back, and I told this to my people. 55 escaped from the 100. 55.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother. 55 I didn't escape right away, but this is not for now. I escaped during the night. 55 escaped. From the 55, 30 were caught, the first week, by Poles, and they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where did you go, when you say you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I escaped to this woman that I brought her the material for three days. But my brother had his mate in a village that he escaped to her, and there was a place for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to know what's going on, so I was in the town. After three days, I went, and I was there with my brother and with the others. But it's not-- this is not the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were there. And after four weeks, I-- our aim was to escape to the woods, and there was already a group of Jews in the woods. Because they were village Jews. They were village Jews that they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods, already.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods, but they didn't go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now, which woods were these? Near?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Jews around our section. There were very dense woods. And I joined them. And we started to buy guns, grenades--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you buy the guns from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From peasants-- through some peasants who were friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you pay them with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We paid them-- we had money. My brother had hidden money, and we went one night, when we were in the camp, and we undug it. And he had, in this time, about $1,200. And this was a very big fortune. And we had the money, and we exchanged, little by little, and we paid this, with the notes I brought back, clothing for my sisters. And we bought guns. We were already 125 Jews in the-- in the-- in the woods. And from the 125 Jews, we started to be killed by this AK. 'AK' is Armia Krajowa. In English it's Home Army. This was the Polish underground which was directed from London, from the Polish government in exile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were killing you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were killing us. They were very antisemitic. And little by little, we-- every time, they killed 10, they killed nine. And we didn't have other Jews to replace them, because all the towns were already Judenrein. There were no Jews. The Jews were already killed. So we didn't have people to replace.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I find out that my friend, who went with me seven years to school-- his name was Stashek Augustin-- that he is the head of this underground. So I said, the only place-- the only way to save us-- if this Stashek Augustin will take me in with my group-- and we will fight the Germans together, and we will not be killed by the AK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made an arrangements. Finally, we met. And we met the 18th of January, 19-- 1944. And I came in to him. We started to make plans. And once we spoke, he was behind my back, he took out a revolver, and he shot me four times. He shot me-- four bullets-- three bullets I got in my neck, and one bullet in my hand. I fell down, dizzy. And I knew that this is it. The minute when he did it, I was very glad. Because I always was afraid that I would be caught by the Germans being alive. So he-- he-- so I was glad that he's going to kill me from the back. But anyway, I was not killed. Laying on the floor, somehow I managed to get a revolver. I killed him, and I killed his brother, right then and there. And I run away with my brother. My brother was with me. And we run away in the big snow, 3-- 3-feet-high snow. 20 degrees below zero. We were naked-- without shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been bleeding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- I didn't know anything. We run away. Only we had our underwear. No shoes. And it took us from 6:00 in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you had no clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had to exchange ours, because our clothes was infested with lice. And he said, take off the clothes-- we'll give you new clothes. And meanwhile this was the shooting. And this was-- it took us from 6:00 in the afternoon till 4:00 in the morning till I reached our group in the woods. I didn't know how to go. I didn't know the way. And, and when you have high snow, you have no roads, you don't know-- you lose your directions. And how we came there is a long story. You have it in the book. Finally, I was-- I find out-- I went to a doctor, the next day, as a Pole. This Polish girl took me to a doctor from the AK. He didn't know-- to a different town. He didn't know who I am. And he told me that I had three bullets and one bullet there. But anyway, I healed. I healed, and then I knew I cannot be with my group, because everybody looked for us. Because my-- they started-- they gave out a prize for my head. Imagine-- I killed two of their leaders-- two of their most prominent-- they were very wealthy people. So I had to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got this [INAUDIBLE]-- I got the paper, and I joined the AK in a different section. My name became Tadeusz Jadach. And I was in the AK as Tadeusz Jadach. Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Pole. I forgot to tell you a other thing, which I will go back. I think this is very important. And everybody knew that I killed him. Everybody-- till today, 50 years later--

speaker: interviewer
text: The AK people knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you joined? With a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. The people from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not this group. Till today, 50 years later, I get letters. I get letters. Still they don't realize how this happened. They don't believe me what I say. They have different versions. And every year come-- came out a different version. When I was in this camp-- and I told you that we saved all this raw material, and we also saved paper. You know, by Jews, you don't throw away religious books. You save it in the attic of the synagogue. When it's enough, you went to take it to the cemetery, and you make a burial.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a whole room. And there came out once-- came in, once, a Scharfuhrer. He's a sergeant from the SS. And he saw the books. And he asked me, what is this? So I told him, holy books. So he was standing in the middle, and there was a small window. In the alleyway, in the alleyway, in the rays from the sun, with the dust from those-- hit him. And he was standing, and he started to yell-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means 'Jehovah, Jehovah, where are you? Why don't you help your children?' And there was not-- no answer. So he said to me, you see-- in German-- you see, your Jehovah is so old that he cannot hear. He's deaf. He cannot help you. And he spat, and he walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this minute, as much as I was not so religious, in this minute I knew that Jehovah was there. And I knew that Jehovah is going to strike him. He should-- became paralyzed. Nothing happened. And he spat down, and he said, Jehovah is dead. But this minute, I said, if I have to survive the war, there is a third legacy. I have to survive to kill this man. Because he insulted my Jehovah. I can have doubt against my god. But he-- this is a drunkard. He was drunk. At-- Five minutes before, he killed one of our inmates. And this man does everything what God forbids. And if he can laugh and insult my-- I have to kill him. Two years later, I shot him. Two years later, I shot this Scharfuhrer. And a few were-- not only him. That we made the raid, and we killed 35 SS men. And in book, if you read it--

speaker: interviewer
text: As an AK member?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a German schar--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you-- you-- as an AK member?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, this was-- no-- from our group. With a Jewish group. Not AK member. They wouldn't know. And I-- and I-- well, you will read the book. Now, if we will have more time, I'll tell you more details. But I want other things to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this way, I promised myself. And I said, God, I will take revenge. And you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did. I said to God, I said, I have quarrels with you. I have quarrels with you. You do a lot of things wrong. But no-- nobody-- no SS man has a right to make fun of you. And I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in the underground. Then later, when, when, when I was in the AK, my brother, with a few-- there were-- there were left only about 15 from the 125. They were killed and killed. And how long did it take, till the end of the war, from that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then the war-- I was liberated-- then I came back. Then, from the AK, there was a other killing, which I don't want to go in in it, because I-- the commandant gave out the order to go and kill three Jews, and I volunteered as a volunteer to go and kill them. This was in the AK. And I wanted to go there, because on the way, I killed my-- the man, and I came, and I took out the Jews. And they survived the war. I couldn't go back to my group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war-- I was liberated-- Did they just want, indiscriminately, three Jews, or three particular Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were hiding--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --by a peasant. So the man who came, who belonged to our group, was from this village. And he came-- he was home for the weekend. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And he came, and he said, that-- I know Jews are being hidden. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he right away gave-- my wife is not there. Go ahead. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gave-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --an order to go-- Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please, can you go and tell her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went, and I volunteered. And when I volunteered, I couldn't go back to my group anymore. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And then the night-- the 28 to 29th of July, we were liberated by the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a-- what? Well, I didn't believe it. First, I didn't believe-- yeah, well, the, the night-- I have to tell you. The night that I was liberated, the night I was lib-- how much do-- how much time do we have?

speaker: interviewer
text: As much time as you need.

speaker: interviewee
text: The night that I was liberated-- the last night-- I was running from our group, which was in German hands. And we had to go through a clearing to the Russian side. And I was going with one of our boys, who was our man, who was a village Jew. And as we went through the clearing, the Russians-- the Germans started to shoot on us. They saw two people. This was during the night. The Russians see that somebody is shooting on those people. They shoot on this side. So we were in  the cross fire. We hid in a-- there was a little brook. We hid in the brook. But anyway, we saw-- this man saw two people on a horse. And he said, I know the people. They are from, from the village. Go and ask them what's going on. We didn't know-- on this side, we-- they shooting and this side, they shooting. We didn't know that it's Russian, German-- we didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I run after them, and I started-- he said, you go, because they know me. I know the people. They don't know you. And I look like a peasant-- no shoes, no boots, a jacket, covered. And, underneath the jacket, I had a belt which-- I had two revolvers, with five hand grenades, with a hatchet, with a bayonet-- everything I had around me covered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran after them, to tell them, to ask them. And I come closer. There are two SS men on the horse. And I come closer. It was too late to come back. And when they saw me-- they didn't know who I am-- they jumped down, took out their revolvers, start to ask me questions in German. I made believe I don't understand. And they asked me who I am, and I started to yell in Polish. I cried. I said, I am from this village. My wife, with the children, went to the woods with the crowds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now they say the Russians are coming. And they say that the Russians are raping women. They will kill my children. What should I do? Help me, help me!

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to them, and they are Germans! And I spoke to them Polish. Then I hear that some-- one of the Germans, of the SS men, starts to translate to the others what I am blabbing. So I knew that he understood something. Then he asked me, in a very broken Polish, from where I am. I said, I am from this village. And I told him the same story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to the other, in German, you know what? We don't have to go to the other side. Because they were afraid, because this was a patrol. Let's take him. And we weill interrogate him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, you have to go with us, to our commanding officer. Here, I will go with him, with all my arsenal around me. And they will take me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So why-- and they said, you go in front of me. And as I start to walk, they remounted, put back their revolvers, and they had rifles. And they said, you go in front, and we will go to our headquarters. As I started to go, I said, where-- where I'm going? I always had a revolver with a long barrel in front in my belt. They didn't know I was in front.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cocked the revolver. And I turned around. In a second, I emptied the seven bullets in them two. And both of them fell down. The minute I start to shoot, again, millions of bullets from both sides. Nobody knew what's going on. And I took the horse as a shield, and I walked over to the other side. I came to the other side. Excuse me. A group from the woods run out, and they surrounded me, and they were Russian partisans. I regularly start to speak Polish. And this was how I was liberated. And they-- the-- I knew that I cannot go back home. Because if I go back home, to my town, I know-- I knew that I would be killed. Because everybody knew that I killed those two big shots. Two-- we survived. We were six left from the 125. Two who were rabbinical students from my town and were with me two years-- we were in camp, in ghetto, two years in the woods-- went back. They said--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, don't go back. They said, why shouldn't I go back? I will be treated as a hero. I survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came back three days after the liberation. They took them to the river, and they shot them. This AK shot them. I know that their name. Stashek Zylinski and Wladyslaw [? Kisce. ?] I wrote it in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is alive. One is alive. He was not-- one is, and one died. And they killed them. Then-- Look, there are other stories. But I want to-- with my mother's sweater. I told you the story with my mother's sweater. She wanted to finish the sweater. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell the story, so it'll be on the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So this story--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell the story about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, when-- I am rushing now. My mother liked to needle-- to make sweaters. And when she made sweaters-- and she didn't have too much wool left, so she made it very fast-- very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked her, why are you rushing so? She said, I want to finish the sweater before I am out of wool. And so the same thing. I am rushing, so long as I have time to push in a other story, a other story. All right. So you were liberated, and you didn't go back to the town. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a second. I didn't go back to the town. Seven other Jews survived from my town, not-- who were not with me in the woods. They were in hiding. And they went back to my town. This AK took the seven Jews into a barn, and they took two members from the AK with rifles to watch on them, to shoot them. And the reason they didn't shot them is because they knew that one more survived. And they went to look for him, to have all eight. There shouldn't be one witness. And they waited for him. Meanwhile, the guards changed. And when the guards changed, one of the guards was working by one of the people of the Jews in a lumber yard. And he felt sorry. And he went, and he told it to a Russian officer. So happens that this Russian officer was a Jew. And he came, and he took them out, and he brought them to Rzeszow, where I already was, in Rzeszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saved them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he saved them. Because they waited till they would have the eighth one. Then they will shoot them. For this reason, I am so anxious to write the book, how Jews were killed after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's held off now, because we're at the end of the tape. And we'll continue in one minute.
